LAW AND MOTION 301, PLAINTIFF DANYELL SANDERS, ON BEHALF OF HERSELF AND ALL OTHERS SIMILARLY SITUATED'S MOTION FOR DISMISSAL WITHOUT PREJUDICE. THIS COURT PREVIOUSLY CONTINUED THE HEARING ON PLAINTIFF'S MOTION TO DISMISS AND ORDERED PLAINTIFF TO PROVIDE A SUPPLEMENTAL DECLARATION FIVE COURT DAYS IN ADVANCE OF THE HEARING. PLAINTIFF HAS NOT DONE SO. THE MOTION IS DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE. COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE COURT'S RULING. JUDGE: CHRISTINE VAN AKEN; CLERK: W. TRUPEK; NOT REPORTED (301/CVA)

Plaintiff'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendant Kaiser Foundation Hospitals' Responses To Special Interrogatory Set No. 1; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Jul-25-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. THOMAS SEGAL, Esq. of Setareh Law Group (Tel: 310-888-7771) for plaintiff Danyell Sanders;
Atty. SEAN T. STRAUSS, Esq. of Seyfarth Shaw LLP (Tel: 415-397-2823) for defendants Kaiser Foundation Hospitals and Kaiser Permanente International.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF DANYELL SANDERS's Motion To Compel Defendant Kaiser Foundation Hospitals' Responses To Special Interrogatory Set No. 1.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

This ruling follows the court's review of the recommendation of Judge Pro Tem Peter C. Catalanotti. At issue is plaintiffs' motion to compel defendant Kaiser's responses to special interrogatory set No. 1; and to requests for production of documents set No. 1.

This is a class action lawsuit against Defendants Kaiser Foundation Hospitals and Kaiser Permanente International for claims such as failure to provide meal periods, failure to provide rest periods, and related claims.

Plaintiffs seek further responses to one interrogatory, SI #1: "Please IDENTIFY each and every PUTATIVE CLASS MEMBER." Plaintiff seeks further responses to one request, RFP #1: "All payroll data, wage records, wage statements, paycheck stubs, or other similar DOCUMENTS that pertain to each and every PUTATIVE CLASS MEMBER during the RELEVANT TIME PERIOD."

Plaintiffs argue they are simply entitled to the names and contact information, citing Williams v. Superior Court, 3 Cal.5th 531 (2017).

Kaiser responds first that the class claims here will be released and precluded upon final approval of settlements in currently pending, related cases. This does not defeat discovery; Kaiser cites no authority in support of its position. The case may or may not settle, and the scope of its releases, as ultimately approved by the court, are a matter of speculation.

Kaiser then notes that serious financial and other privacy interests are implicated in the demand for documents -"All payroll data, wage records, wage statements, paycheck stubs, or other similar DOCUMENTS that pertain to each and every PUTATIVE CLASS MEMBER during the RELEVANT TIME PERIOD."

The parties appear unaware of the usual procedures to obtain information concerning putative class members-even though Williams refers to them as it discusses the privacy interests of the represented people there: "The Belaire-West trial court was correct to order disclosure, subject to employees being given notice of the action, assurance they were under no obligation to talk to the plaintiffs' counsel, and an opportunity to opt out of disclosure by returning an enclosed postcard." Williams, 3 Cal. 5th at 553. Counsel here should review , for example, Belaire-W. Landscape, Inc. v. Superior Ct., 149 Cal. App. 4th 554 (2007). It may well be that contact information should be provided to plaintiffs' counsel, Weil & Brown, et al., California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial 14:135.3 (Rutter: 2022), but putative class members have the right to object to the release of that information, not to speak of the documents now demanded by plaintiffs' counsel, id. at 14:135.6. [I alert counsel to the issues concerning communications with putative class members, before certification. Id. at 14:84.1 ff.]

The motion is denied without prejudice. The parties should confer on a Belaire-West notice procedure.

Court instructs parties to meet and confer on the Belaire notice within 10 days, and, if there is still a dispute, to contact Dept. 611's clerk to arrange a conference call with the Court.

Single order signed as to both discovery motions.

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/CK)

This tentative follows the court's review of the recommendation of Judge Pro Tem Peter C. Catalanotti. At issue is plaintiffs' motion to compel defendant Kaiser's responses to special interrogatory set No. 1; and to requests for production of documents set No. 1.

This is a class action lawsuit against Defendants Kaiser Foundation Hospitals and Kaiser Permanente International for claims such as failure to provide meal periods, failure to provide rest periods, and related claims.

Plaintiffs seek further responses to one interrogatory, SI #1: "Please IDENTIFY each and every PUTATIVE CLASS MEMBER." Plaintiff seeks further responses to one request, RFP #1: "All payroll data, wage records, wage statements, paycheck stubs, or other similar DOCUMENTS that pertain to each and every PUTATIVE CLASS MEMBER during the RELEVANT TIME PERIOD."

Plaintiffs argue they are simply entitled to the names and contact information, citing Williams v. Superior Court, 3 Cal.5th 531 (2017).

Kaiser responds first that the class claims here will be released and precluded upon final approval of settlements in currently pending, related cases. This does not defeat discovery; Kaiser cites no authority in support of its position. The case may or may not settle, and the scope of its releases, as ultimately approved by the court, are a matter of speculation.

Kaiser then notes that serious financial and other privacy interests are implicated in the demand for documents -"All payroll data, wage records, wage statements, paycheck stubs, or other similar DOCUMENTS that pertain to each and every PUTATIVE CLASS MEMBER during the RELEVANT TIME PERIOD."

The parties appear unaware of the usual procedures to obtain information concerning putative class members-even though Williams refers to them as it discusses the privacy interests of the represented people there: "The Belaire-West trial court was correct to order disclosure, subject to employees being given notice of the action, assurance they were under no obligation to talk to the plaintiffs' counsel, and an opportunity to opt out of disclosure by returning an enclosed postcard." Williams, 3 Cal. 5th at 553. Counsel here should review , for example, Belaire-W. Landscape, Inc. v. Superior Ct., 149 Cal. App. 4th 554 (2007). It may well be that contact information should be provided to plaintiffs' counsel, Weil & Brown, et al., California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial 14:135.3 (Rutter: 2022), but putative class members have the right to object to the release of that information, not to speak of the documents now demanded by plaintiffs' counsel, id. at 14:135.6. [I alert counsel to the issues concerning communications with putative class members, before certification. Id. at 14:84.1 ff.]

The motion is denied without prejudice. The parties should confer on a Belaire-West notice procedure.

Court instructs parties to meet and confer on the Belaire notice within 10 days, and, if there is still a dispute, to contact Dept. 611's clerk to arrange a conference call with the Court.

Single order signed as to both discovery motions.

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/CK)
